food_subgroup: Rice dishes with seafood and sauce

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (composite rice dish) used as closest parent; no specific code for salted egg squid rice exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to salted egg squid rice as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of salted egg sotong rice is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 200 g of crispy salted-egg-coated squid with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — cooked white rice (NDB 20444), squid cooked (NDB 15149), salted duck egg yolk, butter, and sauce components; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for comparable rice-with-seafood dishes.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg sotong rice as a composite dish. Values estimated by weighted component method: approximately 50% steamed white rice, 35% deep-fried squid with salted egg yolk sauce (butter, evaporated milk, salted duck egg yolk, curry leaves, chilli), 15% sauce residue. USDA SR Legacy used for rice and squid base values; salted egg yolk contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central egg yolk data (NDB 01128) adjusted for salt-curing. HPB Singapore data for comparable fried rice and seafood rice dishes used as plausibility check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, steamed (GI ~72); composite dish GI dominated by white rice component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg sotong rice. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant white rice component (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). The salted egg yolk sauce and fried squid contribute negligible carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 39.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg yolk and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1680 mg per standard 400 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index white rice base driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish with caution or in reduced portions, as the white rice base carries a high GI of approximately 72 and the estimated glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 39, which is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and cholesterol from salted egg yolk, butter, and deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit consumption of this dish, as the salted egg yolk sauce and deep-frying process contribute significant saturated fat (approximately 3.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (approximately 145 mg per 100 g), both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Sotong Rice is a popular Singaporean-Filipino fusion rice dish consisting of steamed white rice served with crispy deep-fried squid (sotong) coated in a rich, creamy salted duck egg yolk sauce. The sauce is typically prepared by stir-frying salted duck egg yolks with butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, and chilli until a foamy, golden sauce forms, which is then tossed with the battered and deep-fried squid pieces. The dish delivers a complex flavour profile: the squid is crispy on the outside and tender within, while the salted egg sauce is simultaneously savoury, buttery, slightly spicy, and umami-rich. The white rice base provides a neutral, starchy counterpoint to the intensely flavoured topping. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein from the squid (approximately 8.5 g per 100 g), meaningful fat from the egg yolk and butter (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g), and a high carbohydrate load from the white rice (approximately 22 g per 100 g). The sodium content is notably high due to the salted egg yolk, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) and high glycemic load per serving (~39) make it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat and cholesterol content from the salted egg yolk and deep-frying are relevant considerations for cardiovascular health. This dish is widely available at Filipino-style cafes and casual dining establishments in Singapore and is typically priced in the SGD 8–12 range per plate.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg sotong rice as a named composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated using a weighted component method drawing on USDA SR Legacy data for cooked white rice and squid, and USDA FoodData Central data for egg yolk and butter. GI is estimated from the white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no direct approved-source entry for this composite dish was found. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore entry if one becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources (USDA SR Legacy) for this composite dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and carry higher uncertainty than primary-source values. 4. The cuisine tag in the input JSON is listed as Filipino; the dish is served at a Filipino cafe in Singapore and represents a Filipino-Singaporean fusion preparation. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore serving context. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation with permissible ingredients; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. GI estimated at 72 based on white rice component; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
